title: 1,000 Years of Medieval European History in 20 Minutes
description:
in History |   	May 6th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

More than a few medievalôÙists object to the term ãDark Agesã as applied to the periôÙod in which
body:
in History |   	May 6th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

More than a few medievalôÙists object to the term ãDark Agesã as applied to the periôÙod in which they speôÙcialôÙize. That can seem wishôÙful in light of most comôÙparôÙisons between medieval times and the RenaisôÙsance that came afterôÙward, or indeed, the era of the Roman Empire that came before. ConôÙsidôÙer the state of Europe as the fourth cenôÙtuôÙry began: ãThe great cities of antiqôÙuiôÙty were depopôÙuôÙlatôÙed, some left in ruins,ã says the narôÙraôÙtor of the How So video above, telling the stoôÙry of the conôÙtiôÙnenôÙtãs politôÙiôÙcal and linôÙguisôÙtic fragôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion. ãThe Roman transôÙportaôÙtion sysôÙtem decayed, erodôÙing comôÙmuôÙniôÙcaôÙtion and long-disôÙtance trade. Coins vanôÙished, leavôÙing no ecoôÙnomôÙic sysôÙtem to supôÙport proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal armies. LitôÙerôÙaôÙcy plumôÙmetôÙed, cripôÙpling adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtive sysôÙtems. And most notably, peace and secuôÙriôÙty were gone.ã
But thereãs plenôÙty more hisôÙtoôÙry to come thereôÙafter: about a milôÙlenôÙniôÙumãs worth, in fact, which the video covôÙers in a mere twenôÙty minôÙutes. Events of note in that grand sweep include JusôÙtinôÙian Iãs attempt to expand the ByzanôÙtine Empire of the east; the creôÙation and spread of the IslamôÙic caliphate; CharleôÙmagôÙneãs uniôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion of most of westôÙern ChrisôÙtenôÙdom; invaôÙsions by Vikings, MagôÙyars, and MusôÙlim raiders; the rise of casôÙtles and the feuôÙdal sysôÙtem that they came to symôÙbolôÙize; the creôÙation of the Holy Roman Empire; the flourôÙishôÙing of cities and uniôÙverôÙsiôÙties; and the NorôÙman ConôÙquest of EngôÙland, as seen on the Bayeux TapesôÙtry. Thereãs also the unpleasôÙantôÙness of the Black Death, which swept through Europe from the mid-fourôÙteenth to the earôÙly sixôÙteenth cenôÙtuôÙry ã butô as with othôÙer medieval disôÙasôÙters, the plague held the seeds of a civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtionôÙal rebirth.
ãFor some surôÙvivors, the conôÙseôÙquences of the plague were not so grim,ã says the narôÙraôÙtor. ãAs the popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion dropped, land became wideôÙly availôÙable, and the demand for labor rose draôÙmatôÙiôÙcalôÙly.ã PeasôÙants demandôÙed improved conôÙdiôÙtions and revoltôÙed against the rulers who refused; ultiôÙmateôÙly, they ãgained new freeôÙdoms and opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties, and workôÙers enjoyed highôÙer wages. CreôÙativôÙiôÙty and innoôÙvaôÙtion in sciôÙence and culôÙture folôÙlowed, creôÙatôÙing the enviôÙronôÙment in which EuroôÙpean scholôÙars ãdefined the past milôÙlenôÙniôÙum as ãDark Ages,ã and so posiôÙtioned themôÙselves as the tranôÙsiôÙtion between the medieval and modôÙern world.ã Some liken the curôÙrent state of the world to the decline of the Roman Empire; if theyãre corôÙrect, maybe we have anothôÙer RenaisôÙsance to look forôÙward to about 40 genôÙerôÙaôÙtions down the road.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
A Free Yale Course on Medieval HisôÙtoôÙry: 700 Years in 22 LecôÙtures
What Did PeoôÙple Eat in Medieval Times? A Video Series and New CookôÙbook Explain
How EveryôÙthing in a Medieval CasôÙtle Worked, from Its Moats to Its DunôÙgeons
What Sex Was Like in Medieval Times?: HisôÙtoôÙriôÙans Look at How PeoôÙple Got It On in the Dark Ages
How the ByzanôÙtine Empire Rose, Fell, and CreôÙatôÙed the GloôÙriôÙous Hagia Sophia: A HisôÙtoôÙry in Ten AniôÙmatôÙed MinôÙutes
Advice for Time TravôÙelôÙing to Medieval Europe: How to Stay Healthy & Safe, and AvoidôÙing Charges of WitchôÙcraft
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: When Francis Bacon Shocked the Art World: Viewers Were Horrified by His Paintings, But Couldnãt Look Away
description:
in Art, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

A difôÙfiôÙcult childôÙhood and adoôÙlesôÙcence, satôÙuôÙratôÙed with the feelôÙing of being an
body:
in Art, History |   	May 5th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

A difôÙfiôÙcult childôÙhood and adoôÙlesôÙcence, satôÙuôÙratôÙed with the feelôÙing of being an outôÙsider, may or may not conôÙtribute to becomôÙing a great artist. ExpeôÙriôÙencôÙing the social and culôÙturôÙal ferôÙment of Berlin and Paris in the nineôÙteen-twenôÙties probôÙaôÙbly wouldôÙnãt hurt oneãs chances. Nor, sureôÙly, would forôÙmaôÙtive expoôÙsure in such cities to films like MetropôÙoôÙlis, BatôÙtleôÙship Potemkin, and Abel Ganceãsô Napoleon, as well as to the paintôÙings of Pablo PicasôÙso. Going to art school may seem like the natôÙurôÙal choice for any aspirôÙing artist, but thereãs also someôÙthing to be gained from avoidôÙing that acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic sysôÙtem entireôÙly.
These, as galôÙlerist-YoutuôÙber James Payne tells us in the new Great Art Explained video above, are all aspects of the life that proôÙduced FranôÙcis Bacon. As usuôÙal on that series, he proôÙceeds from a sinôÙgle repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtive work, in this case Study after VelûÀzquezãs PorôÙtrait of Pope InnoôÙcent X, from 1953.
If youãve seen that paintôÙing even once, you havenãt forôÙgotôÙten it, and indeed, youãve probôÙaôÙbly seen it again in your nightôÙmares since. To trace the source of its trouôÙbling powôÙer, Payne plunges into the hisôÙtoôÙry of Baconãs harôÙrowôÙing life as well as that of the Irish, EngôÙlish, and EuroôÙpean hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal conôÙtexts in which he lived ã often to its danôÙgerôÙous, chaotôÙic fullest.
Not that any art hisôÙtoôÙriôÙan can ignore the inspiôÙraôÙtion citôÙed right there in the paintôÙingãs title. It is to that sevôÙenôÙteenth-cenôÙtuôÙry Spaniardãs acclaimed porôÙtrait of that head of the Catholic Church (who proôÙnounced the finôÙished work ãtropôÙpo veroã) that Bacon pays twistôÙed, deconôÙstrucôÙtive homage. Yet despite havôÙing been to Rome, he nevôÙer actuôÙalôÙly saw the origôÙiôÙnal; that, as Payne explains, ãwould have meant facôÙing its powôÙer directôÙly.ã Instead, he worked from a small, washed-out ãcopy of a copy,ã all the betôÙter to allow for not just reinôÙvenôÙtion, but also the incorôÙpoôÙraôÙtion of othôÙer scraps of the rapidôÙly expandôÙing mass media of the twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry: the periôÙod, despite the out-of-time qualôÙiôÙty of so much of his art, to which Bacon so thorôÙoughôÙly belonged.

title: Confidence: The Cartoon That Helped America Get Through the Great Depression (1933)
description:
in Animation, History, Music |   	May 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

No more bumôÙminã, letãs all get to workãÎ
ActuôÙalôÙly, hold up a sec. Weãll
body:
in Animation, History, Music |   	May 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

No more bumôÙminã, letãs all get to workãÎ
ActuôÙalôÙly, hold up a sec. Weãll all be hapôÙpiôÙer and more proôÙducôÙtive if we take a moment to start our work day with ConôÙfiôÙdence, a pepôÙpy musiôÙcal aniôÙmaôÙtion from 1933, starôÙring newôÙly electôÙed PresôÙiôÙdentô Franklin Delano RooôÙsevelt and MickôÙey Mouse preôÙcurôÙsor,ô Oswald the Lucky RabôÙbit.ô 
Few Americansãtoday weãd refer to them as the 1%ãcould escape the priôÙvaôÙtions of the Great DepresôÙsion.ô The movies were one indusôÙtry that conôÙtinôÙued to thrive through this dark periôÙod, preôÙciseôÙly because they offered a few hours of respite. No one went to the picôÙtures to see a reflecôÙtion of their own lives. GorôÙgeous gowns, glamôÙorous ManôÙhatôÙtan apartôÙments and romanôÙtic trouôÙble cerôÙtainô to be resolved in hapôÙpy endingsãÎrememberô Mia FarôÙrowãs beleaôÙgueredô waitôÙressô baskôÙing inô the PurôÙple Rose of Cairoãsô reasôÙsurôÙing glow?
GivôÙen the pubôÙlicãs prefôÙerôÙence for escapist fare, direcôÙtor Bill Nolan, the Father of RubôÙber Hose AniôÙmaôÙtion, could have played it safe by glossôÙing over the backôÙstoôÙry that leads Oswald to seek out advice fromô the ComôÙmanôÙder in Chief. Instead, Nolan delivôÙered his joyôÙful carôÙtoon aniôÙmals into nightôÙmare terôÙriôÙtoôÙry, the DepresôÙsion perôÙsonôÙiôÙfied as a cowled Death figôÙure layôÙing waste to the land. Itãs weirdôÙly upsetôÙting to see those hyper-cheerôÙful vinôÙtage barnôÙyard aniôÙmals (and a rogue monôÙkey) underôÙgo this graphôÙicô enerôÙvaôÙtion.
Oh, for some oral historyãIãd love to know how matiôÙnee crowds reactôÙed as Oswald raced screamôÙing before a spinôÙning verôÙtiôÙgo backôÙground, seekôÙing a remôÙeôÙdy for a host of non-carôÙtoon probôÙlems. Irony is a luxôÙuôÙry they didôÙnãt have.
UnsurôÙprisôÙingôÙly,ô the can-do spirôÙit so cenôÙtral to FDRãs New Deal quickôÙly turned Oswaldãs frown upside down. As presôÙiôÙdenôÙtial camôÙpaign promisôÙes go, this oneãs uniqueôÙlyô taiôÙlored to the demands ofô musiôÙcal comôÙeôÙdy. WitôÙness Annie, in which the 32nd presôÙiôÙdent wasô again called uponô to Rex HarôÙriôÙson his way into audiôÙence hearts, this time fromô the wheelôÙchair the creôÙators of ConôÙfiôÙdence didôÙnãt dare show, some forty years earôÙliôÙer.
The diviôÙsion between enterôÙtainôÙment and nation-leadôÙing is pretôÙty perôÙmeôÙable these days, too.
AccordôÙingôÙly, what realôÙly sets this carôÙtoon apart for me is the use of a PresôÙiôÙdenôÙtialôÙly-sancôÙtioned giant syringe as a tool to get DepresôÙsion-era AmerôÙiôÙca back on its feet. A figôÙuôÙraôÙtive injecôÙtion of conôÙfiôÙdence is all well and good, but nothôÙing gets the barnôÙyardô back on its singing, dancôÙing feet like a libôÙerôÙal dose, delivôÙered in the most litôÙerôÙal way.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
A SimôÙple, Down-to-Earth ChristôÙmas Card from the Great DepresôÙsion (1933)
PriôÙvate SnaôÙfu: The World War II ProôÙpaôÙganôÙda CarôÙtoons CreôÙatôÙed by Dr. Seuss, Frank Capra & Mel Blanc
Yale Presents an Archive of 170,000 PhoôÙtographs DocôÙuôÙmentôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion
Great DepresôÙsion CookôÙing: Get BudôÙget-MindôÙed Meals from the Online CookôÙing Show CreôÙatôÙed by 93-Year-Old Clara CanôÙnucôÙciaôÙri
When Al Capone Opened a Soup Kitchen DurôÙing the Great DepresôÙsion: AnothôÙer Side of the LegôÙendary Mobsterãs OperôÙaôÙtion
Ayun HalôÙlôÙiôÙday canãt get enough of that rubôÙber style.ô

title: Why Ancient Egyptian Honey Remains Edible After 3,000 Years
description:
in Film, Food & Drink, History |   	May 5th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
The globôÙal bee popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion comes up in the news every now and again. SomeôÙtime
body:
in Film, Food & Drink, History |   	May 5th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
The globôÙal bee popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion comes up in the news every now and again. SomeôÙtimes weãre assured that the numôÙber is staôÙble or risôÙing; more often, weãre warned about colôÙlapsôÙing colonies and the large-scale ecoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal disôÙasôÙter that could result. As with most high-stakes issues, it can be difôÙfiôÙcult to know what to believe. But even if you lack the time to invest in an underôÙstandôÙing of the sciôÙence behind the comôÙplex conôÙnecôÙtions between apiôÙan and human welôÙfare, you can easôÙiôÙly come to appreôÙciôÙate the imporôÙtance of bees if you learn just how long theyãve played a role in our civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion.
As Elana SpiôÙvack writes at History.com, ãa cave paintôÙing in northôÙeastôÙern Spain depictôÙing a human harôÙvestôÙing honôÙey dates back 7,500 years to the NeolithôÙic periôÙod, accordôÙing to research pubôÙlished in 2021 in the jourôÙnal TraôÙbaôÙjos de PreôÙhisôÙtoôÙria.ã Just last year, a paper in the JourôÙnal of the AmerôÙiôÙcan ChemôÙiôÙcal SociôÙetyô conôÙfirmed that bronze conôÙtainôÙers disôÙcovôÙered in an underôÙground shrine in a sixth-cenôÙtuôÙry-BC Greek setôÙtleôÙment not far from PomôÙpeii conôÙtained a residue of honôÙey. Weãve long known of hieroôÙglyphs from ancient Egypt that depict bees and the keepôÙing thereôÙof; ãaccordôÙing to a 2022 paper in the jourôÙnal AniôÙmals, the use of honôÙeyôÙbees in the Nile ValôÙley can be traced to the earôÙliôÙest years of the EgyptôÙian kingôÙdom.ã
Here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry, most of us regard honôÙey as nothôÙing more than a relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly healthy sweetôÙenôÙer. In ancient Egypt, too, it was used to improve the taste of their bread and beer, but it was also put to imporôÙtant medôÙical uses. ãBecause itãs so thick, rejects any kind of growth and conôÙtains hydroôÙgen perôÙoxôÙide, it creôÙates the perôÙfect barôÙriôÙer against infecôÙtion for wounds,ã writes SmithôÙsonôÙianãs Natasha GeilôÙing. ãThe ancient EgypôÙtians used medôÙiôÙcôÙiôÙnal honôÙey regôÙuôÙlarôÙly, makôÙing ointôÙments to treat skin and eye disôÙeases.ã They may not have been the first to do so, givôÙen that the earôÙliôÙest known uses of honôÙey are recordôÙed on SumerôÙian clay tablets, but they took respect for the stuff to a whole new levôÙel, describôÙing honôÙeyôÙbees as origôÙiôÙnatôÙing from the tears of their sun god Re (forôÙmerôÙly known in the EngôÙlish-speakôÙing world as Ra).
That parôÙticôÙuôÙlar piece of mytholôÙoôÙgy is recordôÙed on some EgyptôÙian papyri; othôÙers reveal how much honôÙey was rationed to workôÙers, at least those employed directôÙly by the Pharaoh. In those days, the subôÙstanceãs goldôÙen colôÙor reflectôÙed its dearôÙness, and it seems that comôÙmon laborôÙers and their famôÙiôÙlies could go a lifeôÙtime withôÙout ever tastôÙing a spoonôÙful themôÙselves. Today, of course, we take it for grantôÙed that we can go down to the superôÙmarôÙket and cheapôÙly buy an econôÙoôÙmy-size tub of honôÙey that nevôÙer goes bad. But then, ancient EgyptôÙian honôÙey has nevôÙer gone bad either: thanks to the very same chemôÙiôÙcal and bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal propôÙerôÙties that made it useôÙful for healôÙing,ô the sealed jars of it remain theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcalôÙly ediôÙble even after 3,000 years. DrizôÙzle it on some genôÙuineô Greek yogurt, and youãve got a large swath of the hisôÙtoôÙry of civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion in breakôÙfast form.
via Boing Boing/SmithôÙsonôÙian
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

A 3,000-Year-Old Painterãs Palette from Ancient Egypt, with Traces of the OrigôÙiôÙnal ColôÙors Still In It

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: How Fatinha Ramos Channels ãVisual Activismã in Her Richly Layered Illustrations
description: ãTo me, being a visual activist means I only illustrate stories that resonate with me deeply, by giving voice to minorities or social situations t
body:
ãTo me, being a visual activist means I only illustrate stories that resonate with me deeply, by giving voice to minorities or social situations that need to be addressed,ã says Fatinha Ramos. ãIt is the only way I can truly connect with others.ã
Based in Antwerp, the Portuguese artist and illustrator is well-known for blending analog and digital techniques to create rich, emotive compositions. Collaborating with clients like The New York Times, The Washington Post, Tate, and Scientific American, among many others, Ramos has cultivated a keen eye for storytelling through her distinctive visual language.

Recent partnerships include the Anne Frank Museum and MoMA, the latter of which commissioned the artist to illustrate an essay on her experience being compared to Frida Kahlo. Ramos was born with osteogenesis imperfecta, commonly called brittle bone disease, which spurred a childhood spent in and out of hospitals. Drawing and art-making quickly became a preferred pastime, allowing her to transport herself from such clinical settings.
This adolescent hobby stuck, and Ramos worked as an art director in advertising and publishing for 12 years before venturing out on her own. Boasting an impressive list of clients and collaborators, she considers her practice to be an antidote to stereotypical narratives, whether related to the climate crisis, sexism, racism, or the dire lack of empathy that seems rampant in todayãs world.
ãI want to move away from the narrative that turns artists with disabilities into symbols of resilience,ã she tells MoMA. ãCreativity does not happen despite limitations but through them. Art should broaden how we see the worldãand that includes how we see bodies, too.ã
At the moment, Ramos is only accepting select illustration clients as she focuses on a graphic novel and her fine art practice, including a series of anatomical glass sculptures based on brittle bone disease. You can explore more of her practice on her website and Instagram.

schema.headline: How Fatinha Ramos Channels ãVisual Activismã in Her Richly Layered Illustrations

title: Luscious Hair Sculptures Sprout Like Branches in a Symbiotic Exhibition
description: A visit to Lincoln Park or the Garfield Park Conservatory is one of the outings Chicagoans rarely pass up, particularly when we need some reprieve
body:
A visit to Lincoln Park or the Garfield Park Conservatory is one of the outings Chicagoans rarely pass up, particularly when we need some reprieve from all the concrete and steel. Two beloved green spaces in the city, these spots boast oases blanketed in verdant foliage even in the depths of winter and house an array of specimens not native to the Midwest. 
For artists Merryn Omotayo Alaka and Sam Frûˋsquez, the immersive nature of a conservancy, with plants above and below and all around, became a central point for a collaborative project. Your Birth is My Birth presents the duoãs synthetic hair sculptures, which suspend from the ceiling of Jane Lombard Gallery and splay across the wooden floor like organic growths. Alaka and Frûˋsquez describe the exhibition as a sort of ãKanekalon forest,ã referring to the brand behind the luscious material.
Installation view of ãYour Birth is My Birth.ã Photo by Adam Reich
Five different ãspeciesã emerge in the space, including Listening Roots,ô Hearing Bells,ô Mother & Child,ô Stacking Pearls, andô Umbra Pods. Dark, dyed locks and domed shapes are throughlines, although each takes on a distinctive form. The series are influenced by epiphytes, non-parasitic plants that make their homes on a host specimen. Think orchids, cacti, moss, and kelp.
Surging upward from lily pad-shaped discs suctioned to the floorboards, the largest sculpture is part of Listening Roots, which tethers singular shoots to a central form. This connection between smaller piecesãlike the feather-duster-shaped Stacking Pearlsãand more comprehensive structures recurs throughout the exhibition, gesturing toward an intimate and intentional symbiosis.
Several works also reference genetics and whatãs passed down through generations, as mirrored forms emerge within the same vertical tendril. ãSimilar to an epiphyte and its host tree, these sculptural works have their own life cycles evoking systems of dependence and exchange, where one form sustains from another,ã says a statement.
Your Birth is My Birth is on view through June 13. Explore more from Alaka and Frûˋsquez on Instagram.
ãStacking Pearl (Adolescent) Iã (2026), Kanekalon hair and steel support, 24 x 24 inches
Installation view of ãYour Birth is My Birth.ã Photo by Adam Reich
Detail of ãUmbra Pods Iã (2026), Kanekalon hair and steel support, 45 x 27 inches
Installation view of ãYour Birth is My Birth.ã Photo by Adam Reich
Detail of ãUmbra Pods IIIã (2026), Kanekalon hair and steel support, 45 x 27 inches
Installation view of ãYour Birth is My Birth.ã Photo by Adam Reich

title: Wander through Adrienna Matzeg's Embroidered, Late-Night City Explorations
description: When Adrienna Matzeg embarked on a trip to Kyoto, Tokyo, and Seoul in July 2025, she encountered intense midsummer heat and humidity, which led he
body:
When Adrienna Matzeg embarked on a trip to Kyoto, Tokyo, and Seoul in July 2025, she encountered intense midsummer heat and humidity, which led her to exploring some of the citiesã nooks and crannies in the dark, when it was cooler. 
Illuminated storefronts and signage characterize the artistãs late-night runs to convenience stores, markets, and other features of these hubsã sprawling  urban fabric. ãIn her textile embroidery work, however, the energy of the city falls away,ã says a statement from Abbozzo Gallery, which presents her forthcoming solo exhibition, After Hours. ãWhat remains are quiet scenes that left an imprint, tactile snapshots as a record of those summer nights.ã
ãLate Night Snackã
After Hours represents an evolution of Matzegãs travel-related works, centered around embroidered vignettes of snacks and roadside attractions that have a snapshot-like, diaristic quality. Brightly lit facades and bold displays seem to float on the surface of black linen, with the addition of a small plate of hanami dangoãthe distinctive pink, white, and green rice dumpling snack on a stickãdrawing us close to the artistãs experience.
After Hours runs from May 8 to 30 in Toronto. See more on the artistãs Instagram.
